      One-day lookback
   =20
 =20

There are different ways to buy a stock index. You can just pay cash to buy=
 all the stocks in the index. You can buy an exchange-traded fund that hold=
s all the stocks in the index. Or you can do an equity index swap: You sign=
 a contract with a bank in which the bank promises to pay you the return on=
 the index over some term. If the index is at 100 today, and you do a $10 m=
illion one-year swap with a bank, and then=C2=A0the index is at 108 in a ye=
ar, then the bank pays you $800,000 (the index=E2=80=99s 8% return on the $=
10 million notional amount). If the index is at 93 in a year, you pay the b=
ank $700,000.
The swap is approximately like the bank=C2=A0lending=C2=A0you the $10 milli=
on to buy the index: You don=E2=80=99t put up the $10 million upfront,
  [1]
 but you get back the return on $10 million worth of stock.
  [2]
 The bank charges you for that synthetic loan; the terms of the swap contra=
ct will say:
=09The bank pays you any positive return on the index.=09You pay the bank a=
ny negative return on the index, plus=C2=A0an interest rate.=C2=A0

And so when you call up a bank to do a swap on some index, the bank will qu=
ote you a price consisting of the interest rate that you will pay =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0=E2=80=9C3-month US dollar Libor plus 70 basis points,=E2=80=9D for i=
nstance.
  [3]
 Or vice versa: You could=C2=A0sell=C2=A0a swap to the bank, if you want to=
 get short the index; then the bank would pay you the interest rate and you=
=E2=80=99d pay the bank the return on the index.=C2=A0
This makes the equity index swap roughly equivalent to=C2=A0buying the ETF:=
 Either way, you make money when the index goes up, lose money when the ind=
ex goes down, and pay some cost of funding to get the money, real or synthe=
tic, that you use to buy the index.
At some high level of generality, buying=C2=A0an index using a swap and buy=
ing it using an ETF should be economically equivalent. But if you are an in=
dex trader at an investment bank,=C2=A0your entire job is to find places wh=
ere they are not equivalent. Your job is to do the arbitrage=C2=A0=E2=80=94=
=C2=A0buying and selling the same index in two different ways =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0in such a way that you make a little bit of money. One simple way is: If=
 you can borrow money at 2% to buy the ETF for cash, and then you can sell =
a one-year swap at a 2.1% financing rate, then you make a 0.1% profit.
  [4]
 Your profit is not about the index going up or down; you have hedged that =
out. (You bought the index for cash and sold it on swap.) Your profit is ab=
out the different interest rate.
There are better trades. Here is a good=C2=A0one, from a  Commodity Futures=
 Trading Commission enforcement action against Goldman Sachs Group Inc. yes=
terday. Disclosure: I used to work at Goldman, I sold derivatives, and whil=
e I did not do or witness anything precisely like this, uh, let=E2=80=99s j=
ust say that this enforcement action brought a tear to this old derivatives=
 structurer=E2=80=99s eye. This is good stuff!
  [5]
=C2=A0Sure sure sure sure sure it is apparently illegal, but I cannot help =
but admire it.
The trick starts with the fact that in an equity index swap, the short part=
y pays the long party the return on the index over the =E2=80=9Cstrike pric=
e,=E2=80=9D meaning roughly =E2=80=9Cthe price of the index today.=E2=80=9D=
 So if the index is at 100 today, we can do a $10 million swap struck at 10=
0, and then if the index is at 108 in a year I will pay you=C2=A0$800,000, =
etc.=C2=A0
But customarily the strike price is actually the price of the index tomorro=
w: We sign the swap contract today, and then we set the strike price tomorr=
ow, and then the swap pays the return over the strike price in a year or wh=
atever the term is.  The CFTC explains:
Equity index swaps traditionally strike on a =E2=80=9CT+1=E2=80=9D basis. T=
his means that the =E2=80=9Cstrike=E2=80=9D price, or =E2=80=9Cinitial pric=
e,=E2=80=9D of the swap equals the index value published the day after the =
parties agree to the trade. This one-day lag ensures that none of the compo=
nents of the index that will form the initial valuation have priced at the =
time the swap is agreed upon, reducing the likelihood of potential informat=
ion asymmetries regarding the projected index values.
So if you buy a swap today, ordinarily it starts tomorrow, to avoid any inf=
ormation asymmetries.
About those information asymmetries. Goldman=E2=80=99s trick=C2=A0was to tr=
ade T+0 swaps, that is, swaps that were struck based on=C2=A0that day=E2=80=
=99s=C2=A0index level. This doesn=E2=80=99t matter that much, if you trade =
US stock indexes in the US: If you trade an S&P 500 swap at 2 p.m., and it =
strikes based on that day=E2=80=99s S&P 500 closing level, you don=E2=80=99=
t know what that level will be. But Goldman traded T+0 swaps, in New York, =
on non-US indexes that closed before New York did:
Almost all the =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D equity index swaps at issue in th=
is matter were swaps tied to the MSCI Europe Australasia and Far East (=E2=
=80=9CEAFE=E2=80=9D) Index (=E2=80=9CEAFE Index Swaps=E2=80=9D). The MSCI E=
AFE Index tracks a basket of stocks from Japan, Europe, Hong Kong, Singapor=
e, New Zealand, and Australia, among others. The MSCI EAFE Index level (or,=
 index price) is published once per day, in the early evening, New York tim=
e (=E2=80=9CIndex Settlement Value=E2=80=9D). It is calculated by MSCI, usi=
ng a formula based on the closing prices of the index=E2=80=99s component s=
tocks. The foreign exchanges on which the component stocks trade, however, =
close hours before New York exchanges do=E2=80=94some before the New York t=
rading day even begins. In other words, most of the component prices used t=
o calculate the Index Settlement Value are stale=E2=80=94at least several h=
ours old=E2=80=94by the time the Index Settlement Value gets published in N=
ew York.
If you trade a T+0 swap on the MSCI EAFE Index at 2 p.m. in New York, (1) t=
he index price is not published yet, but=C2=A0(2) you already pretty much k=
now what it will be, because all the stocks have closed already.
Also, though, you have a head start on knowing what=C2=A0tomorrow=E2=80=99s=
=C2=A0index price will be:
Though most of the component stocks in the EAFE Index trade on exchanges th=
at close well before the New York markets do, there are other =E2=80=9Cprox=
y=E2=80=9D instruments that generally track the market value of the compone=
nts of the EAFE index and trade throughout the New York trading day. For ex=
ample, there is an exchange traded fund (=E2=80=9CETF=E2=80=9D) that tracks=
 the MSCI EAFE Index. It trades on the NYSE throughout the New York trading=

AFE index settlement price for Tuesday will be, say, 2,000 =E2=80=94=C2=A0a=
 price that will be published Tuesday evening, but that reflects the index =
level as of Tuesday morning. But the ETF on that index trades all day in Ne=
w York, and you can=C2=A0look at the trading price of the=C2=A0ETF=C2=A0at =
2 p.m., and it might be=C2=A0above or below 2,000. If the broad market is r=
allying and people expect European and Asian stocks to trade up, then the E=
TF might trade at, say, 2,050. The CFTC says:
The result is twofold: (1) since most of the markets in which the underlyin=
g equities traded closed hours before the Index Settlement Value was publis=
hed, sophisticated parties with=C2=A0the wherewithal and resources to acces=

 of=E2=80=94what the daily Index Settlement Value would be hours before tha=

t value of the components of the index, as reflected in proxy instruments l=
ike the EAFE ETF, can=E2=80=94and did=E2=80=94 diverge from the projected I=
ndex Settlement Value. For example, during the New York trading day, the ET=
F and other proxies could be trading above (at a =E2=80=9Cpremium=E2=80=9D =
to) the projected Index Settlement Value. Or, the ETF and other proxies cou=
ld be trading below (at a =E2=80=9Cdiscount=E2=80=9D to) the projected Inde=
x Settlement Value.
There is a simple arbitrage: Sell the ETF at 2,050, buy the swap at 2,000, =
and collect the 50 points of difference. Trade the same index in two differ=
ent ways at two different prices.
And that=E2=80=99s what Goldman did.=C2=A0It went out to swaps customers an=
d said =E2=80=9Chey we=E2=80=99ll pay you Libor plus 55 basis points for a =
regular-way T+1 swap,=C2=A0or, if you want, we can pay you Libor plus 90 ba=
sis points for a T+0 swap, that=E2=80=99s a good deal huh?=E2=80=9D And som=

For example, on January 21, 2016, Goldman told a client that Goldman could =
=E2=80=9Cbid=E2=80=9D for a nine-month EAFE Index Swap. Specifically, Goldm=
an said: =E2=80=9Cnormal T[+]1 is 3mL+55 bid (+60 mid). for today's strike =
can show 3mL+91.=E2=80=9D By bidding the same-day swap at a rate that appea=

ap. It was not. Indeed, at the time, the EAFE index proxies were trading at=

 client would be selling the equity leg to Goldman at a below-market rate. =
Although Goldman offered the client a higher interest rate than the client =
would have received by trading a T+1 swap, Goldman=E2=80=99s higher same-da=
y interest rate did not adequately compensate the client for the disadvanta=
ge on the equity leg. =E2=80=A6
Goldman would only solicit or agree to a =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D swap wh=

man, conversely, would be selling or buying the equity leg at a level it co=

et, the =E2=80=9Cpreferential=E2=80=9D interest rates that Goldman offered =
clients to entice them to transact same-day did not fully compensate the cl=

And:
As a result, =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D swaps were particularly lucrative f=
or Goldman. For example, on August 25, 2015, Goldman offered to enter a sam=

=E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D at this seemingly preferential rate. The strike =

The client thought it was getting a good deal, eh? Thought Goldman was just=

Was this=C2=A0fraud? I mean, not exactly? The relevant pieces of informatio=

say =E2=80=9Chere is our price for a normal T+1 swap and here is our better=

=E2=80=99s, just, you know:
To be sure, the market prices of the index underliers, as well as projectio=

=E2=80=99re pulling a fast one on Goldman Sachs, you might be right, but ma=

Earlier this year, Johnson & Johnson=E2=80=99s baby-powder division=C2=A0wa=

ing J&J, alleging that the talc caused cancer. J&J has always denied these =
claims, but it lost some of these cases and had to pay huge damages. More c=
ases were coming, and to get out ahead of them, J&J decided to go to bankru=
ptcy court. It used a  =E2=80=9CTexas two-step=E2=80=9D merger to separate =
out its talc liabilities into a new subsidiary called LTL Management LLC, a=
nd then had LTL file for bankruptcy.
There are obvious benefits of this for J&J. For one thing, the bankruptcy f=
iling would let J&J handle all of its talc claims in one case, instead of l=
itigating against different plaintiffs in different courts. Also J&J presum=
ably expected the result to be cheaper: Bankruptcy courts are in the busine=
ss of working out negotiated plans to pay all similar claimants the same am=
ount, while going to a bunch of jury trials in different courts ran the ris=
k of getting a bunch of different=C2=A0multibillion-dollar verdicts. Juries=
 sometimes award huge damages to send companies a message, while bankruptcy=

Still it is important not to=C2=A0overstate=C2=A0the benefits of the Texas =
two-step for J&J. The goal was to get into bankruptcy court and pay plainti=
ffs a negotiated amount, not to stiff them entirely. You=C2=A0could imagine=
 using this approach to stiff the talc claimants entirely: Maybe J&J could =
have put all of its talc liabilities into LTL, given LTL no assets, filed f=
or bankruptcy and said =E2=80=9Csorry everyone, your lawsuits are now again=
st LTL, which has no money.=E2=80=9D Lots of people did, and I guess still =
do, imagine that that=E2=80=99s what J&J actually did. But it didn=E2=80=99=
t. Instead, J&J promised to provide financial support to LTL to pay any pla=
usible amount of talc damages: If the LTL bankruptcy case ended up awarding=
 talc plaintiffs $10 billion or $30 billion or $60 billion of damages, J&J =
would write a check for that amount to LTL, and LTL would have enough cash =
to pay the claims. J&J, which is a $400 billion public company with AAA cre=
dit ratings, agreed to provide this money in a Funding Agreement; the amoun=
t was capped at something like $61.5 billion, which let us assume was more =
than the talc plaintiffs will get.
So LTL filed for bankruptcy in New Jersey, and a bankruptcy judge concluded=
 that this was all in good faith and allowed. Some talc claimants appealed,=
 though, and in January an appeals court threw LTL out of bankruptcy. We  d=
iscussed the case, and the opinion, at the time.
The appeals court did=C2=A0not throw out the bankruptcy because it conclude=
d that J&J was acting in bad faith or trying to stiff the claimants or anyt=

 nearly insolvent or otherwise in financial distress, and LTL wasn=E2=80=99=
t,=C2=A0because J&J had promised to pay it up to $61.5 billion to pay all t=
hese claims.=C2=A0The  court wrote:

We cannot agree LTL was in financial distress when it filed its Chapter 11 =
petition. The value and quality of its assets, which include a roughly $61.=
5 billion payment right against J&J and New Consumer, make this holding unt=
enable. =E2=80=A6
From these facts=E2=80=94presented by J&J and LTL themselves=E2=80=94we can=
 infer only that LTL, at the time of its filing, was highly solvent with ac=
cess to cash to meet comfortably its liabilities as they came due for the f=
oreseeable future. It looks correct to have implied, in a prior court filin=
g, that there was not =E2=80=9Cany imminent or even likely need of [it] to =
invoke the Funding Agreement to its maximum amount or anything close to it.=
=E2=80=9D

That is, the problem is not that J&J put its talc liabilities into a box, u=
ndercapitalized the box to stiff claimants, and then had the box file for b=
ankruptcy. The problem is that J&J put its talc liabilities into a box,=C2=
=A0overcapitalized=C2=A0the box to make sure that it was able to pay claima=
nts, and then had the box file for bankruptcy. The court concluded that the=
 overcapitalized box was not allowed to file for bankruptcy.
You see the solution, right?
No, I=E2=80=99m kidding. The obvious, ironic solution=C2=A0is for J&J and L=
TL to tear up the Funding Agreement and have LTL file for bankruptcy again =
with no money in it. =E2=80=9CSorry,=E2=80=9D J&J would say to the talc pla=
intiffs, =E2=80=9Cbut the court said we couldn=E2=80=99t file for bankruptc=
y with enough money to pay you, so we=E2=80=99re not gonna pay you.=E2=80=
=9D There are problems with this solution, though. The point of the LTL str=
ucture is to come to some sort of negotiated settlement with enough plainti=

A slight oversimplification of the rules is that in, in the US, it is illeg=

You could have a model of carbon emissions that goes like this:
=09As the world becomes more concerned about carbon emissions, it will beco=
me increasingly expensive, illegal, undesirable=C2=A0or impossible to drill=
, sell or use oil.=09Unless we find some way to make all the carbon emissio=
ns disappear, in which case oil is fine.

In that model, the biggest beneficiaries of carbon-capture technology will =
be oil companies, because in that model oil is basically a complement to ca=
rbon capture technology. If carbon capture works, you can sell lots of oil;=
 if not, you can=E2=80=99t.
So if you are building promising carbon-capture technology, the way to mone=
tize it is to buy stock in oil companies, or just to be an oil company. The=
 Wall Street Journal  reports on Occidental Petroleum Corp.:

It is spending more than $1 billion to build the first in a planned fleet o=
f plants using direct-air capture to pull the CO2 out of the air, a budding=

ntives included in the climate package President Biden signed into law last=

er metric ton.=C2=A0
Chief Executive Vicki Hollub, who has the blessing of the company=E2=80=99s=
 largest investor, Warren Buffett, said the plan will help it reach net-zer=
o emissions on all its operations, its own energy use and its customers=E2=
=80=99 use of its products, by 2050, and allow it to keep investing in oil =
extraction. =E2=80=A6

t to keep up its investments in oil and gas. She warned that underinvestmen=
t in fossil fuels, which she says will be needed for years even amid the br=
oader transition to clean energy, will lead to a scarcity of supplies. In c=
ontrast, she said, other oil majors such as BP PLC and Shell PLC have shrun=
k their oil segment and invested in renewables.
Oil companies will have to find ways to remove as much carbon dioxide as th=
ey emit =E2=80=9Cif they want to be the last producer standing in the world=
,=E2=80=9D Ms. Hollub said.

In the abstract, it is a hard problem to=C2=A0get people to pay for clean a=
ir and low carbon emissions: Clean air and stable temperatures benefit ever=
yone, and it=E2=80=99s hard to charge them all; there are free-rider proble=
ms. A lot of modern financial and regulatory engineering =E2=80=94 emission=
s regulations,=C2=A0carbon-credit trading, ESG investing, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0goes into fixing this problem, putting a price on carbon emissions so th=
at someone will pay for fixing them. Saying =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re gonna ba=
n oil companies unless oil companies find a way to capture carbon=E2=80=9D =
is in some ways a simpler approach.

       =20
  [1] You put up some margin, there are collateral terms, etc.; sometimes t=
his   lands people in trouble but it doesn=E2=80=99t concern us here.


  [2] One way for this to work is that the bank buys $10 million of actual =
stock or ETF, with its own money, and pays you the return. The bank is hedg=
ed, long the stock and short the swap. In single-stock swaps it is customar=
y to think of the trade this way, "the bank buys the stock and pays you the=
 return via a swap," though that's not necessarily what happens; the bank c=
ould hedge with another swap, or not hedge at all.


  [3] The London interbank offered rate is being phased out as a benchmark =
interest rate, so I guess now they=E2=80=99d quote you the Secured Overnigh=
t Financing Rate, but in 2015 and 2016, the period relevant here, it was Li=
bor.


  [4] I am ignoring various risks and complications here, including credit =
risk, capital, etc., but never mind.


  [5] It is loosely related to the concept in the section header here, the =
=E2=80=9Cone-day lookback.=E2=80=9D In various contexts you can have an opt=
ion where you have the right to exercise the option on any day and get the =
price that day. The option contract might say =E2=80=9CBank can exercise th=
e option as of some date (the =E2=80=98Exercise Date=E2=80=99) by deliverin=
g a notice by close of business on the Exercise Date.=E2=80=9D *Or*, it mig=
ht say =E2=80=9CBank can exercise the option as of some date (the =E2=80=98=
Exercise Date=E2=80=99) by delivering a notice by close of business on the =
following business day.=E2=80=9D Those sentences are almost the same! The d=
ifference seems boring and administrative, a matter of convenience, somethi=
ng a lawyer or back-office person would care about but not a trader. Nope! =
Having a one-day lookback on an option =E2=80=94 deciding on Tuesday whethe=
r to exercise at Monday=E2=80=99s price =E2=80=94 can be incredibly valuabl=
e; it is an extra little option on an option. (Consider the   Bed Bath & Be=
yond Inc. convertible preferred stock sale, where a ton of the economics we=
re baked into the 10-day lookback on the conversion price.) There are, uh, =
times when the derivatives salesperson at the bank pays more attention to t=
his sentence than the customer does.


  [6] There *is* a Funding Agreement in which a LTL=E2=80=99s direct parent=
 company, a J&J subsidiary named Johnson & Johnson Holdco (NA) Inc., agrees=
 to fund LTL=E2=80=99s liabilities. I don=E2=80=99t know enough about Holdc=
o=E2=80=99s capitalization to know how valuable that funding is, but presum=
ably it is capitalized thinly *enough* that LTL can argue it is in financia=
l distress. Meanwhile the Support Agreement (with the J&J public-company pa=
rent) backstops that agreement, but only when LTL is in bankruptcy.


  [7] Roughly: When a fund company uses research and pays for it with commi=
ssions, the fund=E2=80=99s *clients* pay the commissions (trading costs are=
 charged to the fund) but arguably the fund=E2=80=99s *managers* get the be=
nefits of the research (it is part of their expenses of managing the fund).=
 When the fund company has to pay for the research directly, either the man=
agement company eats the cost, or it explicitly and transparently passes it=
 on to its customers.

adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">One-day lookback</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are different ways to buy a stock index.=
 You can just pay cash to buy all the stocks in the index. You can buy an e=
xchange-traded fund that holds all the stocks in the index. Or you can do a=
n equity index swap: You sign a contract with a bank in which the bank prom=
ises to pay you the return on the index over some term. If the index is at =
100 today, and you do a $10 million one-year swap with a bank, and then=C2=
=A0the index is at 108 in a year, then the bank pays you $800,000 (the inde=
x=E2=80=99s 8% return on the $10 million notional amount). If the index is =
at 93 in a year, you pay the bank $700,000.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The swap is approximately like the bank=C2=A0<=
em>lending=C2=A0</em>you the $10 million to buy the index: You don=E2=80=99=
t put up the $10 million upfront,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footn=
ote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !i=
mportant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;"=
>
<span>[1]</span>
</a> but you get back the return on $10 million worth of stock.<a class=3D"=
footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !impo=
rtant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a> The bank charges you for that synthetic loan; the terms of the swap co=
ntract will say:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The bank pays you any positive return =
on the index.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You pay the bank any=
 negative return on the index, <em>plus=C2=A0</em>an interest rate.=C2=A0</=
li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so when you call up a bank to do a swap on=
 some index, the bank will quote you a price consisting of the interest rat=
e that you will pay =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9C3-month US dollar Libor plus 70=
 basis points,=E2=80=9D for instance.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#f=
ootnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; borde=
r-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: non=
e !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-blo=
ck;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> Or vice versa: You could=C2=A0<em>sell=C2=A0</em>a swap to the bank, i=
f you want to get short the index; then the bank would pay you the interest=
 rate and you=E2=80=99d pay the bank the return on the index.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This makes the equity index swap roughly equiv=
alent to=C2=A0buying the ETF: Either way, you make money when the index goe=
s up, lose money when the index goes down, and pay some cost of funding to =
get the money, real or synthetic, that you use to buy the index.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At some high level of generality, buying=C2=A0=
an index using a swap and buying it using an ETF should be economically equ=
ivalent. But if you are an index trader at an investment bank,=C2=A0<em>you=
r entire job is to find places where they are not equivalent</em>. Your job=
 is to do the arbitrage=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0buying and selling the same ind=
ex in two different ways =E2=80=94=C2=A0in such a way that you make a littl=
e bit of money. One simple way is: If you can borrow money at 2% to buy the=
 ETF for cash, and then you can sell a one-year swap at a 2.1% financing ra=
te, then you make a 0.1% profit.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footno=
te-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !im=
portant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a> Your profit is not about the index going up or down; you have hedged t=
hat out. (You bought the index for cash and sold it on swap.) Your profit i=
s about the different interest rate.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are better trades. Here is a good=C2=A0o=
ne, from a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3113143=
0.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2Z0Yy5nb3YvUHJlc3NSb29tL1ByZXNzUmVsZWFzZXMvODY4NS=
0yMw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf4bd6dbc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Commodity Futures Trading =
Commission enforcement action</a> against Goldman Sachs Group Inc. yesterda=
y. Disclosure: I used to work at Goldman, I sold derivatives, and while I d=
id not do or witness anything precisely like this, uh, let=E2=80=99s just s=
ay that this enforcement action brought a tear to this old derivatives stru=
cturer=E2=80=99s eye. This is good stuff!<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Sure sure sure sure sure it is apparently <em>illegal</em>, but I=
 cannot help but admire it.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The trick starts with the fact that in an equi=
ty index swap, the short party pays the long party the return on the index =
over the =E2=80=9Cstrike price,=E2=80=9D meaning roughly =E2=80=9Cthe price=
 of the index today.=E2=80=9D So if the index is at 100 today, we can do a =
$10 million swap struck at 100, and then if the index is at 108 in a year I=
 will pay you=C2=A0$800,000, etc.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But customarily the strike price is actually t=
he price of the index <em>tomorrow</em>: We sign the swap contract today, a=
nd then we set the strike price tomorrow, and then the swap pays the return=
 over the strike price in a year or whatever the term is. <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2Z=
0Yy5nb3YvbWVkaWEvODM3Ni9lbmZnb2xkbWFub3JkZXIwNDEwMjMvZG93bmxvYWQ/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB050f9f8c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> The CFTC explains</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Equity index swaps traditionally s=
trike on a =E2=80=9CT+1=E2=80=9D basis. This means that the =E2=80=9Cstrike=
=E2=80=9D price, or =E2=80=9Cinitial price,=E2=80=9D of the swap equals the=
 index value published the day after the parties agree to the trade. This o=
ne-day lag ensures that none of the components of the index that will form =
the initial valuation have priced at the time the swap is agreed upon, redu=
cing the likelihood of potential information asymmetries regarding the proj=
ected index values.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So if you buy a swap today, ordinarily it star=
ts tomorrow, to avoid any information asymmetries.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">About those information asymmetries. Goldman=
=E2=80=99s trick=C2=A0was to trade T+0 swaps, that is, swaps that were stru=
ck based on=C2=A0<em>that day=E2=80=99s=C2=A0</em>index level. This doesn=
=E2=80=99t matter that much, if you trade US stock indexes in the US: If yo=
u trade an S&P 500 swap at 2 p.m., and it strikes based on that day=E2=
=80=99s S&P 500 closing level, you don=E2=80=99t know what that level w=
ill be. But Goldman traded T+0 swaps, in New York, on non-US indexes that c=
losed before New York did:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Almost all the =E2=80=9Csame-day=
=E2=80=9D equity index swaps at issue in this matter were swaps tied to the=
 MSCI Europe Australasia and Far East (=E2=80=9CEAFE=E2=80=9D) Index (=E2=
=80=9CEAFE Index Swaps=E2=80=9D). The MSCI EAFE Index tracks a basket of st=
ocks from Japan, Europe, Hong Kong, Singapore, New Zealand, and Australia, =
among others. The MSCI EAFE Index level (or, index price) is published once=
 per day, in the early evening, New York time (=E2=80=9CIndex Settlement Va=
lue=E2=80=9D). It is calculated by MSCI, using a formula based on the closi=
ng prices of the index=E2=80=99s component stocks. The foreign exchanges on=
 which the component stocks trade, however, close hours before New York exc=
hanges do=E2=80=94some before the New York trading day even begins. In othe=
r words, most of the component prices used to calculate the Index Settlemen=
t Value are stale=E2=80=94at least several hours old=E2=80=94by the time th=
e Index Settlement Value gets published in New York.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you trade a T+0 swap on the MSCI EAFE Index=
 at 2 p.m. in New York, (1) the index price is not <em>published </em>yet, =
but=C2=A0(2) you already pretty much know what it will be, because all the =
stocks have closed already.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also, though, you have a head start on knowing=
 what=C2=A0<em>tomorrow=E2=80=99s=C2=A0</em>index price will be:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Though most of the component stock=
s in the EAFE Index trade on exchanges that close well before the New York =
markets do, there are other =E2=80=9Cproxy=E2=80=9D instruments that genera=
lly track the market value of the components of the EAFE index and trade th=
roughout the New York trading day. For example, there is an exchange traded=
 fund (=E2=80=9CETF=E2=80=9D) that tracks the MSCI EAFE Index. It trades on=
 the NYSE throughout the New York trading day and reflects the market=E2=80=
=99s view of the real-time value of the basket of stocks in the correspondi=
ng index=E2=80=94despite the fact that the markets in which the underlying =
stocks trade may be closed.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So at=C2=A02 p.m. on Tuesday in New York, you =
can calculate that the MSCI EAFE index settlement price for Tuesday will be=
, say, 2,000 =E2=80=94=C2=A0a price that will be published Tuesday evening,=
 but that reflects the index level as of Tuesday morning. But the ETF on th=
at index trades all day in New York, and you can=C2=A0look at the trading p=
rice of the=C2=A0ETF=C2=A0<em>at 2 p.m.</em>, and it might be=C2=A0above or=
 below 2,000. If the broad market is rallying and people expect European an=
d Asian stocks to trade up, then the ETF might trade at, say, 2,050. The CF=
TC says:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The result is twofold: (1) since m=
ost of the markets in which the underlying equities traded closed hours bef=
ore the Index Settlement Value was published, sophisticated parties with=C2=
=A0the wherewithal and resources to access relevant market data could know=
=E2=80=94or have a very good approximation of=E2=80=94what the daily Index =
Settlement Value would be hours before that publication; and (2) during the=
 New York trading day, the real-time market value of the components of the =
index, as reflected in proxy instruments like the EAFE ETF, can=E2=80=94and=
 did=E2=80=94 diverge from the projected Index Settlement Value. For exampl=
e, during the New York trading day, the ETF and other proxies could be trad=
ing above (at a =E2=80=9Cpremium=E2=80=9D to) the projected Index Settlemen=
t Value. Or, the ETF and other proxies could be trading below (at a =E2=80=
=9Cdiscount=E2=80=9D to) the projected Index Settlement Value.</p></blockqu=
ote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is a simple arbitrage: Sell the ETF at 2=
,050, buy the swap at 2,000, and collect the 50 points of difference. Trade=
 the same index in two different ways at two different prices.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And that=E2=80=99s what Goldman did.=C2=A0It w=
ent out to swaps customers and said =E2=80=9Chey we=E2=80=99ll pay you Libo=
r plus 55 basis points for a regular-way T+1 swap,=C2=A0<em>or</em>, if you=
 want, we can pay you Libor plus 90 basis points for a T+0 swap, that=E2=80=
=99s a good deal huh?=E2=80=9D And some customers said yes:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For example, on January 21, 2016, Goldman told=
 a client that Goldman could =E2=80=9Cbid=E2=80=9D for a nine-month EAFE In=
dex Swap. Specifically, Goldman said: =E2=80=9Cnormal T[+]1 is 3mL+55 bid (=
+60 mid). for today's strike can show 3mL+91.=E2=80=9D By bidding the same-=
day swap at a rate that appeared to be 31 basis points over the [<em>pre-tr=
ade mid-market mark, i.e., the fair market price</em>]=C2=A0for the T+1 swa=
p, Goldman gave the client the impression that the same-day swap was a bett=
er deal for the client than the T+1 swap. It was not. Indeed, at the time, =
the EAFE index proxies were trading at a premium to the projected Index Set=
tlement Value=E2=80=94meaning that the client would be selling the equity l=
eg to Goldman at a below-market rate. Although Goldman offered the client a=
 higher interest rate than the client would have received by trading a T+1 =
swap, Goldman=E2=80=99s higher same-day interest rate did not adequately co=
mpensate the client for the disadvantage on the equity leg. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Goldman would only solicit or agree to a =E2=
=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D swap when the client would be buying or selling the=
 equity leg underwater and Goldman, conversely, would be selling or buying =
the equity leg at a level it could immediately cash in for a profit through=
 the index proxy instruments. Yet, the =E2=80=9Cpreferential=E2=80=9D inter=
est rates that Goldman offered clients to entice them to transact same-day =
did not fully compensate the clients for the disadvantage at which the clie=
nt would start the equity leg.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As a result, =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=
=80=9D swaps were particularly lucrative for Goldman. For example, on Augus=
t 25, 2015, Goldman offered to enter a same-day swap with a client at an in=
terest rate that, according to internal communications, was 15 basis points=
 above what the client would have received in an analogous =E2=80=9CT+1=E2=
=80=9D swap. The client agreed to transact =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D at th=
is seemingly preferential rate. The strike price the client received on the=
 equity leg, however, was at least 60 basis points below market at or aroun=
d the time of the agreement. As a result, Goldman traders estimated that th=
ey had earned a $1,620,000 profit that day on this swap and the correspondi=
ng index proxy trades alone. The client, for its part, thought it was getti=
ng a good deal; it did not understand why Goldman was offering the high int=
erest rate.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The client thought it was getting a good deal,=
 eh? Thought Goldman was just paying a little extra interest out of the goo=
dness of its heart?=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Was this=C2=A0<em>fraud</em>? I mean, not exac=
tly? The relevant pieces of information =E2=80=94=C2=A0the closing levels o=
f the stocks in the index, the trading levels of the index ETF =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0were all public and pretty easy to get and calculate. Goldman wasn=E2=80=
=99t exactly=C2=A0tricking anyone into doing T+0 swaps. It wasn=E2=80=99t c=
rossing the =E2=80=9C1=E2=80=9D out of the swap confirmation and writing in=
 =E2=80=9C0.=E2=80=9D It would openly say =E2=80=9Chere is our price for a =
normal T+1 swap and here is our better price for a T+0 swap, don=E2=80=99t =
you want that one instead?=E2=80=9D It=E2=80=99s, just, you know:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To be sure, the market prices of t=
he index underliers, as well as projections of the end-of-day Index Settlem=
ent Value based on then-prevailing market prices of underliers (or, for und=
erliers that had closed for the day, the closing prices), as well as the pr=
icing of the index proxy instruments are publicly available. Parties with t=
he resources and wherewithal to do so could, like Goldman, see when the pro=
xy instruments were trading at a premium or a discount to the projected Ind=
ex Settlement Value and, therefore, whether the projected Index Settlement =
Value was above or below the real-time market value of the components of th=
e Index. Goldman=E2=80=99s clients, however=E2=80=94particularly, the ones =
to whom Goldman repeatedly returned to execute =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D s=
waps=E2=80=94rarely demonstrated such sophistication and often appeared to =
believe they were receiving an advantageous interest rate, with no strings =
attached.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yes yes yes. If you are a DERIVATIVES SALESPER=
SON AT GOLDMAN SACHS, and you have a customer=C2=A0who APPEARS=C2=A0TO BELI=
EVE THAT HE IS=C2=A0RECEIVING AN ADVANTAGEOUS INTEREST RATE, FROM GOLDMAN S=
ACHS, WITH NO STRINGS ATTACHED<em>,=C2=A0</em>you sure=C2=A0<em>will=C2=A0<=
/em>repeatedly return to that customer=C2=A0to execute trades. That=E2=80=
=99s a real=C2=A0good customer! The customer who thinks that Goldman Sachs =
are charitable idiots who are giving him a good deal for no reason is going=
 to be getting a lot of calls from Goldman Sachs. =E2=80=9CHey bud, got ano=
ther great deal for you,=E2=80=9D his Goldman salesperson will giggle to hi=
m, a dozen times a day.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Look, I do not want to be too generous about G=
oldman=E2=80=99s cleverness, or too ungenerous about its sharp elbows, but,=
 just, if you think that you=E2=80=99re pulling a fast one on Goldman Sachs=
, you might be right, but maybe double-check to be sure? If you think that =
you are=C2=A0<em>repeatedly pulling the same fast one on Goldman Sachs</em>=
, man, you=E2=80=99d better be really sure.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, right, it=E2=80=99s not exactly fraud, bu=
t it=E2=80=99s a little shady. Goldman definitely=C2=A0<em>pushed=C2=A0</em=
>these trades:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On another occasion, in January 2016, Goldman =
personnel asked a client: =E2=80=9Cany interest in [EAFE swaps]? We have an=
 axe if you=E2=80=99re looking to put on more short exposure.=E2=80=9D When=
 the client, who had never traded =E2=80=9Csame-day=E2=80=9D before respond=
ed with interest to sell $90 million notional, the Goldman salesperson quot=
ed:</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For Tomorrow=E2=80=99s strike: 3mL+50 bid (PTM=
M +53)<br />for TODAY=E2=80=99s strike: 3mL+100</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When the client responded with interest in the=
 T+1 strike, the Goldman salesperson replied: =E2=80=9Ctomorrow=E2=80=99s s=
trike? You=E2=80=99re not interested in today=E2=80=99s at the much better =
level?=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94explicitly suggesting that the client would be bett=
er off trading same-day. Yet, the salesperson failed to disclose the disadv=
antage=E2=80=94that the client=E2=80=99s equity leg would start underwater,=
 at a below-market strike price.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And did not explain <em>why=C2=A0</em>it liked=
 them so much:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Furthermore, communications show t=
hat Goldman personnel believed that the less the clients understood about t=
he economics of the same-day swaps, the more profit Goldman could make. As =
a Goldman trader told a Goldman salesperson, disclosing more detail about s=
ame-day swaps =E2=80=9Cmakes it easier for them to squeeze us; we usually j=
ust say we have an axe.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The CFTC concludes that Goldman=E2=80=99s comp=
arison of its T+0 prices to the standard T+1 mid-market price was misleadin=
g, and that all of this violated Goldman=E2=80=99s obligation =E2=80=9Cto c=
ommunicate with counterparties in a fair and balanced manner based on princ=
iples of fair dealing and good faith.=E2=80=9D Which, yes, fine. It is a bi=
t of a gray area, but if you are a big bank swaps dealer, and you are smart=
er than your clients, you cannot=C2=A0<em>abuse=C2=A0</em>that advantage to=
o much; this apparently went too far.=C2=A0</p>

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">J&J</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Earlier this year, Johnson & Johnson=E2=80=
=99s baby-powder division=C2=A0was thrown out of bankruptcy court for not b=
eing bankrupt enough. What happened is that J&J sold baby powder with t=
alc in it, and=C2=A0people started suing J&J, alleging that the talc ca=
used cancer. J&J has always denied these claims, but it lost some of th=
ese cases and had to pay huge damages. More cases were coming, and to get o=
ut ahead of them, J&J decided to go to bankruptcy court. It used a <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmFrZXJib3R0cy5jb20vdGhvdWdodC1sZWFkZXJzaGlwL3B1YmxpY2F0aW9ucy8yM=
DIzL2ZlYnJ1YXJ5L3RoZS10aGlyZC1jaXJjdWl0LXJlamVjdHMtdGV4YXMtdHdvLXN0ZXAtYmFu=
a3J1cHRjeS1zdHJhdGVneQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBea070db3" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> =E2=80=
=9CTexas two-step=E2=80=9D merger</a> to separate out its talc liabilities =
into a new subsidiary called LTL Management LLC, and then had LTL file for =
bankruptcy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are obvious benefits of this for J&J=
. For one thing, the bankruptcy filing would let J&J handle all of its =
talc claims in one case, instead of litigating against different plaintiffs=
 in different courts. Also J&J presumably expected the result to be che=
aper: Bankruptcy courts are in the business of working out negotiated plans=
 to pay all similar claimants the same amount, while going to a bunch of ju=
ry trials in different courts ran the risk of getting a bunch of different=
=C2=A0multibillion-dollar verdicts. Juries sometimes award huge damages to =
send companies a message, while bankruptcy courts kind of don=E2=80=99t.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still it is important not to=C2=A0<em>overstat=
e=C2=A0</em>the benefits of the Texas two-step for J&J. The goal was to=
 get into bankruptcy court and pay plaintiffs a negotiated amount, not to s=
tiff them entirely. You=C2=A0could <em>imagine </em>using this approach to =
stiff the talc claimants entirely: Maybe J&J could have put all of its =
talc liabilities into LTL, given LTL no assets, filed for bankruptcy and sa=
id =E2=80=9Csorry everyone, your lawsuits are now against LTL, which has no=
 money.=E2=80=9D Lots of people did, and I guess still do, imagine that tha=
t=E2=80=99s what J&J actually did. But it didn=E2=80=99t. Instead, J&am=
p;J promised to provide financial support to LTL to pay any plausible amoun=
t of talc damages: If the LTL bankruptcy case ended up awarding talc plaint=
iffs $10 billion or $30 billion or $60 billion of damages, J&J would wr=
ite a check for that amount to LTL, and LTL would have enough cash to pay t=
he claims. J&J, which is a $400 billion public company with AAA credit =
ratings, agreed to provide this money in a Funding Agreement; the amount wa=
s capped at something like $61.5 billion, which let us assume was more than=
 the talc plaintiffs will get.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So LTL filed for bankruptcy in New Jersey, and=
 a bankruptcy judge concluded that this was all in good faith and allowed. =
Some talc claimants appealed, though, and in January an appeals court threw=
 LTL out of bankruptcy. We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=
ljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDEtMzEvbWF0dC1sZXZpbmUtam9obnNvbi1qb2huc29uLXMtam5qLWJhbmtyd=
XB0Y3ktZGlkbi10LXdvcms_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQxMTIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1l=
bWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDExJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1=
tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8d070b26" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> discussed the cas=
e</a>, and the opinion, at the time.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The appeals court did=C2=A0<em>not </em>throw =
out the bankruptcy because it concluded that J&J was acting in bad fait=
h or trying to stiff the claimants or anything like that. Instead, its obje=
ction was that LTL wasn=E2=80=99t bankrupt enough: Bankruptcy, the court sa=
id, is for companies that are insolvent or nearly insolvent or otherwise in=
 financial distress, and LTL wasn=E2=80=99t,=C2=A0<em>because J&J had p=
romised to pay it up to $61.5 billion to pay all these claims.=C2=A0</em>Th=
e <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cyLmNhMy51c2NvdXJ0cy5nb3Yvb3BpbmFyY2gvMjIyMDAzcC5wZGY/607f07c=
eef4b8524a319a23cBa0f00681" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" =
target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important=
; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> court wrote</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We cannot agree LTL was in financial distress =
when it filed its Chapter 11 petition. The value and quality of its assets,=
 which include a roughly $61.5 billion payment right against J&J and Ne=
w Consumer, make this holding untenable. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">From these facts=E2=80=94presented by J&J =
and LTL themselves=E2=80=94we can infer only that LTL, at the time of its f=
iling, was highly solvent with access to cash to meet comfortably its liabi=
lities as they came due for the foreseeable future. It looks correct to hav=
e implied, in a prior court filing, that there was not =E2=80=9Cany imminen=
t or even likely need of [it] to invoke the Funding Agreement to its maximu=
m amount or anything close to it.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is, the problem is not that J&J put i=
ts talc liabilities into a box, undercapitalized the box to stiff claimants=
, and then had the box file for bankruptcy. The problem is that J&J put=
 its talc liabilities into a box,=C2=A0<em>overcapitalized=C2=A0</em>the bo=
x to make sure that it was able to pay claimants, and then had the box file=
 for bankruptcy. The court concluded that the overcapitalized box was not a=
llowed to file for bankruptcy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You see the solution, right?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No, I=E2=80=99m kidding. The obvious, ironic s=
olution=C2=A0is for J&J and LTL to tear up the Funding Agreement and ha=
ve LTL file for bankruptcy again with no money in it. =E2=80=9CSorry,=E2=80=
=9D J&J would say to the talc plaintiffs, =E2=80=9Cbut the court said w=
e couldn=E2=80=99t file for bankruptcy with enough money to pay you, so we=
=E2=80=99re not gonna pay you.=E2=80=9D There are problems with this soluti=
on, though. The point of the LTL structure is to come to some sort of negot=
iated settlement with enough plaintiffs to get a bankruptcy court to sign o=
ff on it (and bind the rest of the plaintiffs), and you can=E2=80=99t get a=
 negotiated settlement with no money for victims. People will just keep sui=
ng and arguing that the bankruptcy is in bad faith, the Texas two-step merg=
er is invalid, etc., and some of them will probably win. You can=E2=80=99t =
really get away with this approach, even if it sort of technically works.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still the basic idea is right: J&J has to =
have LTL file for bankruptcy again, but with less money, so that it looks m=
ore bankrupt. And so, Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Steven Church and Jef Feeley <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMDUvai1qLWJlZ2luc=
y1hdWRhY2lvdXMtcmV0dXJuLXRvLWZhaWxlZC1jYW5jZXItc2V0dGxlbWVudC10YWN0aWM_Y21w=
aWQ9QkJEMDQxMTIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3N=
sZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDExJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cB401d91b6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> reported last week</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Tuesday, just hours after a judge officiall=
y ended that first effort, J&J tried the gambit again. The company put =
the same subsidiary that had been tossed out of bankruptcy court back into =
Chapter 11, this time with a plan to pay $8.9 billion to resolve the decade=
s-old cancer claims. The move has already drawn the ire of some victim lawy=
ers and raised eyebrows among legal scholars, who are asking why this time =
will be any different. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the new case, J&J argues this one is di=
fferent because they have support from many more cancer victims. That shows=
 the company is acting in good faith, and the new case also meets a test se=
t by the appeals court in Philadelphia, lawyers for the bankrupt subsidiary=
 said in court documents filed Tuesday.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is the (new) <a href=3D"https://link.mail=
.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly9kbS5lcGlxMTEuY29tL2=
Nhc2UvbHRsL2RvY2tldHM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4ad30c01" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> bankruptc=

ptcy.=C2=A0</em>That is, before it files for=C2=A0bankruptcy, LTL has limit=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These new financing arrangements r=
esolve the concerns that led the Third Circuit to conclude the Debtor=E2=80=

=E2=80=99s balance sheet and liquidity were no longer available to the Debt=
or before the filing of this second chapter 11 case. J&J=E2=80=99s supp=
ort, which was always intended to facilitate, not frustrate, a bankruptcy f=
iling by the Debtor now serves its intended purpose.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Get it? LTL might not have enough money to pay=

=A0<em>won=E2=80=99t </em>give it enough money to pay all the talc claims. =

=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyou said LTL wasn=E2=80=99t bankrupt enough, so we mad=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A slight oversimplification of the rules is th=
at in, in the US, it is illegal for brokers to charge clients money directl=
y for investment research, while in the EU it is=C2=A0illegal=C2=A0for brok=
ers=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>to charge clients money directly for investment =
research. In the US, brokers provide research for =E2=80=9Cfree,=E2=80=9D a=
nd clients compensate them for the research by doing trades with them and p=
aying commissions for the trades. The EU dislikes the <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZXNtYS5=

=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> potential conflicts of interest in that model</a>,<a class=3D=
"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorat=
ion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00000=
0; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !imp=
ortant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a> so in the 2018 Markets in Financial Instruments Directive it=C2=A0bann=
ed=C2=A0it: Clients have to pay for research explicitly. In the US, though,=
 charging people for research might make you an =E2=80=9Cinvestment adviser=
,=E2=80=9D requiring separate registration with the Securities and Exchange=
 Commission and creating awkward new fiduciary obligations.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For the last five years the SEC has sort of wa=
ived this conflict, telling US=C2=A0brokers that they can comply with Mifid=
 (by charging European clients directly for research) without registering a=
s investment advisers. But now that is ending. The <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2=
NvbnRlbnQvMjQwMGI1MjAtYWZhMy00NWVjLTk3NjctYTM1ODA1YjRmOThh/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cBff061268" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;"> Financial Times reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After July 3, US broker-dealers are set to los=
e the protection of a five-year =E2=80=9Cno action=E2=80=9D letter from the=
 Securities and Exchange Commission that covered them against having to reg=
ister as investment advisers. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Without that protection they face a choice of =
registering, moving research teams into already registered affiliates, or p=
otentially cutting off clients bound by Mifid regulations from research pro=
duced in the US.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CBanks are talking to each other and t=
o their clients. It=E2=80=99s already causing disruption,=E2=80=9D said one=
 person involved in the behind-the-scenes discussions. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Banks have resisted investment adviser status =
because it would restrict them from some activities including principal tra=
ding, and could hinder their ability to offer bespoke research, according t=
o people with experience of the rules. The costs and complications of reorg=
anising to register would depend on each bank=E2=80=99s individual arrangem=
ents, but were not insurmountable, they added.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is a strange little conflict? One way to re=
ad the situation is that the SEC doesn=E2=80=99t mind forcing brokers to re=
gister their research arms as investment advisers with fiduciary duties to =
the research customers.=C2=A0The SEC has historically been a little suspici=
ous of sell-side investment research, and the main suspicion is=C2=A0that s=
ometimes research analysts seem to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbu=
siness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You could have a model of carbon emissions tha=
t goes like this:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">As the world becomes more concerned a=
bout carbon emissions, it will become increasingly expensive, illegal, unde=
sirable=C2=A0or impossible to drill, sell or use oil.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">Unless we find some way to make all the carbon emission=
s disappear, in which case oil is fine.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In that model, the biggest beneficiaries of ca=
rbon-capture technology will be oil companies, because in that model oil is=
 basically a complement to carbon capture technology. If carbon capture wor=
ks, you can sell lots of oil; if not, you can=E2=80=99t.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So if you are building promising carbon-captur=
e technology, the way to monetize it is to buy stock in oil companies, or j=
ust to be an oil company. The Wall Street Journal <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31131430.257449/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9=
hcnRpY2xlcy9vY2NpZGVudGFsLXBsYW5zLXRvLXN1Y2stY2FyYm9uLWZyb20tdGhlLWFpcnNvLW=
l0LWNhbi1rZWVwLXB1bXBpbmctb2lsLTI5OTBjNWE_bW9kPWl0cF93c2omcnU9eWFob28/607f0=
7ceef4b8524a319a23cB10d9824d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope=
" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> reports on Occidental Petroleum Corp=
.</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is spending more than $1 billion to build t=
he first in a planned fleet of plants using direct-air capture to pull the =
CO2 out of the air, a budding technology with fuzzy economics. Bolstering t=
he move are generous tax incentives included in the climate package Preside=
nt Biden signed into law last year that cover up to 45% of Occidental=E2=80=
=99s expected initial costs per metric ton.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Chief Executive Vicki Hollub, who has the bles=
sing of the company=E2=80=99s largest investor, Warren Buffett, said the pl=
an will help it reach net-zero emissions on all its operations, its own ene=
rgy use and its customers=E2=80=99 use of its products, by 2050, and allow =
it to keep investing in oil extraction. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ms. Hollub told The Wall Street Journal in Aug=
ust that Occidental=E2=80=99s efforts on carbon capture and on becoming a n=
et-zero emitter would allow it to keep up its investments in oil and gas. S=
he warned that underinvestment in fossil fuels, which she says will be need=
ed for years even amid the broader transition to clean energy, will lead to=
 a scarcity of supplies. In contrast, she said, other oil majors such as BP=
 PLC and Shell PLC have shrunk their oil segment and invested in renewables=
.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Oil companies will have to find ways to remove=
 as much carbon dioxide as they emit =E2=80=9Cif they want to be the last p=
roducer standing in the world,=E2=80=9D Ms. Hollub said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the abstract, it is a hard problem to=C2=A0=
get people to pay for clean air and low carbon emissions: Clean air and sta=
ble temperatures benefit everyone, and it=E2=80=99s hard to charge them all=
; there are free-rider problems. A lot of modern financial and regulatory e=
ngineering =E2=80=94 emissions regulations,=C2=A0carbon-credit trading, ESG=
 investing, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0goes into fixing this problem, putting a pr=
ice on carbon emissions so that someone will pay for fixing them. Saying =
=E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re gonna ban oil companies unless oil companies find a =
way to capture carbon=E2=80=9D is in some ways a simpler approach.</p>
